title: SO2 Test Protocol: Blockchain Integrity & Scalability
subtitle: Gas determinism, consensus & scalability

blocks.text: SO2 validates the Quorum QBFT permissioned ledger across four dimensions: gas determinism, consensus correctness, throughput scaling, and hardware resource behavior.

A critical part of SO2 is verifying that the blockchain behaves correctly under adversarial conditions. Three fault injection scenarios were executed on the live 4-node cluster by deliberately crashing, disconnecting, or partitioning nodes. Each scenario tests a different failure mode from the BFT literature: crash faults (fail-stop), omission faults (silent node), and network partitions (symmetric split). The table below summarizes each scenario, its injection method, and the expected observation.

blocks.caption: QBFT Fault Injection Scenarios
rows: Block halt on both sides; post-heal convergence

blocks.text: Gas determinism (SO2-T1) verifies that each smart contract operation consumes a fixed, predictable amount of gas regardless of when or how many times it is invoked. The Coefficient of Variation normalizes gas consumption variance to the mean, where a CV near zero confirms that the system is metering costs deterministically rather than varying with chain state or block timing.

blocks.label: Coefficient of Variation (Gas Determinism)
blocks.note: Normalizes dispersion to the mean; allows cross-workload comparison.

blocks.text: Throughput scaling (SO2-T3) tests whether the system's observed transaction throughput increases proportionally with the send-rate up to the point where the consensus mechanism becomes the bottleneck. The Coefficient of Determination (R²) quantifies this proportionality: an R² close to 1.0 in the pre-saturation region confirms linear scaling, while a sharp R² drop identifies the saturation knee.

blocks.label: Coefficient of Determination (Scaling)
blocks.note: Quantifies proportionality between send-rate and observed throughput.

blocks.text: Two tests, consisting of Consensus Ceiling (SO2-T4) and Medium Agnosticism (SO2-T5), require comparing independent groups of throughput observations. The Mann–Whitney U test is used instead of the independent-samples t-test because throughput distributions under saturation are typically skewed. SO2-T4 uses it to compare ingestion-phase TPS against consensus-phase TPS, confirming the serialization bottleneck. SO2-T5 uses it to confirm that wired and wireless mediums produce statistically equivalent throughput.

Hardware metrics (SO2-T6 through SO2-T8) are interpreted descriptively; no inferential test is applied. Their purpose is to confirm that hardware is not the binding constraint at the consensus-limited throughput ceiling. Peak CPU utilization, memory headroom, GC stop-the-world pause durations, heap growth trends, and disk/network I/O utilization are reported to rule out hardware bottlenecks.
